coredata.dc:title: Effect of various mass ratios of graphene quantum dots doping on the photoelectric performance of ZnSe-GQDs nanocomposites
coredata.dc:creator: Lei, Yun
Wu, Yuncui
Jiang, Zicong
Ouyang, Zhong
Hu, Jiaxin
Lin, Yuanyuan
Du, Peng
Zou, Bingsuo
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized via the Hummers' method and the hydrothermal cutting process. The lateral size of GQDs was 50 nm, and the thickness was 6 nm. The cubic phase ZnSe nanoparticles were obtained by the hydrothermal method, and the diffraction peaks could be ascribed to the (111), (220), (311), (400), (331) and (422) crystal planes of ZnSe. By controlling the addition amount of GQDs with 0.04%, 0.06%, 0.08%, and 0.1%, ZnSe-GQDs composites were prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. When testing composite materials, we could clearly obtain two lattice stripes belonging to GQDs and ZnSe in HRTEM, respectively. ZnSe-GQDs composites could also be found the characteristic peaks representing GQDs and ZnSe at 284 nm and 446 nm on the UV–Vis absorption spectra, respectively. The effect of different GQDs contents on the photoelectric property of ZnSe-GQDs film was investigated. The optimal photocurrent density of ZnSe-0.08 wt% GQDs was three times larger than that of pure ZnSe. The semicircle radius of electrochemical impedance spectrum was reduced with the addition of GQDs gradually, which proved that the appropriate amount of GQDs could reduce the charge transfer resistance of ZnSe in this composite.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs contents
ZnSe nanoparticles
ZnSe-GQDs nanocomposites
Photoelectric performance

Morphology analysis of GQDs and ZnSe-GQDs composites
FT-IR analysis of ZnSe and ZnSe-GQDs composites
UV–Vis analysis of ZnSe and ZnSe-GQDs composites
PL analysis of ZnSe and ZnSe-GQDs composites
Zeta potential analysis of ZnSe, GQDs, and ZnSe-GQDs composites
I-t analysis of ZnSe and ZnSe-GQDs composites
EIS of ZnSe and ZnSe-GQDs composites

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of pure ZnSe (a) and ZnSe-GQDs composites (b).
AFM graph (a) of GQDs and height profile graphs (b, c) of GQDs, TEM image of GQDs(d) and HRTEM image of ZnSe-GQDs composites (e).
High-resolution C 1s (a), O 1s (b), Se 3d (c), and Zn 2p (d) X-ray photoelectron spectra of ZnSe-GQDs.
FT-IR spectra of pure ZnSe (a), ZnSe-GQDs (b), GQDs (c) and rGO (d).

UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQDs (a), ZnSe-GQDs composites (b) (ZnSe, ZnSe-0.04 wt%GQDs, ZnSe-0.06 wt%GQDs, ZnSe-0.08 wt%GQDs, ZnSe-0.1 wt%GQDs), and Tauc's plots (c) of pure ZnSe, ZnSe-0.04 wt%GQDs, ZnSe-0.06 wt%GQDs, ZnSe-0.08 wt%GQDs, ZnSe-0.1 wt%GQDs.
PL of ZnSe, ZnSe-GQDs (a) and Photoluminescence spectra of GQDs with the excitation at 420–480 nm (b).
Zeta potential of GQDs, ZnSe, ZnSe-GQDs.
Transient photocurrent responses of ZnSe-GQDs composites (a) (ZnSe, ZnSe-0.04 wt%GQDs, ZnSe-0.06 wt%GQDs, ZnSe-0.08 wt%GQDs, ZnSe-0.1 wt%GQDs) and I–V of pure ZnSe and ZnSe-0.08 wt%GQDs (b).
EIS of ZnSe, ZnSe-0.04 wt%GQDs,ZnSe-0.06 wt%GQDs,ZnSe-0.08 wt%GQDs,ZnSe-0.1 wt%GQDs (a) and Bode plot of pure ZnSe and ZnSe-0.1 wt%GQDs (b).
Photoelectron transfer diagram of ZnSe-GQDs composites.

head.ce:title: Effect of various mass ratios of graphene quantum dots doping on the photoelectric performance of ZnSe-GQDs nanocomposites

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Resources and Environmental Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Non-ferrous Metals and Materials
Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MOE Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Non-ferrous Metals and Materials, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning, 530004, Peoples R China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of resources and environmental engineering, Wuhan University of Technology, 122 Luoshi Road, Wuhan, Hubei, 430070, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of resources and environmental engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized via the Hummers' method and the hydrothermal cutting process. The lateral size of GQDs was 50 nm, and the thickness was 6 nm. The cubic phase ZnSe nanoparticles were obtained by the hydrothermal method, and the diffraction peaks could be ascribed to the (111), (220), (311), (400), (331) and (422) crystal planes of ZnSe. By controlling the addition amount of GQDs with 0.04%, 0.06%, 0.08%, and 0.1%, ZnSe-GQDs composites were prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. When testing composite materials, we could clearly obtain two lattice stripes belonging to GQDs and ZnSe in HRTEM, respectively. ZnSe-GQDs composites could also be found the characteristic peaks representing GQDs and ZnSe at 284 nm and 446 nm on the UV–Vis absorption spectra, respectively. The effect of different GQDs contents on the photoelectric property of ZnSe-GQDs film was investigated. The optimal photocurrent density of ZnSe-0.08 wt% GQDs was three times larger than that of pure ZnSe. The semicircle radius of electrochemical impedance spectrum was reduced with the addition of GQDs gradually, which proved that the appropriate amount of GQDs could reduce the charge transfer resistance of ZnSe in this composite.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon materials consisted of two-dimension (2D) graphene nanosheets, one-dimensional (1D) carbon nanotubes and zero-dimensional (0D) graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which was considered as an ideal conductive carrier for semiconductor nanocrystals [1–4]. In addition to this, GQDs were made by reducing the transverse size of the graphene sheet to nanometer size [5–7]. As a new type of 0D nanoparticles with a size less than 20 nm [8,9], GQDs had attracted much attention due to the broad absorption band-gap, the high surface area, and excellent electron-transfer properties. The combination of GQDs with semiconductors was widely studied, and these composites could be used for the preparation of various sensors like temperature sensors, chemical sensors, optical sensors and so on [10–17]. There was also some research on the combination of GQDs with the semiconductor to enhance its photoelectric performance [18–20]. Zinc selenide (ZnSe) with a direct band-gap was of particular interesting in various II-VI semiconductors because of its wide range of applications [21], such as displays, sensors nonlinear optical devices, and infrared windows [22–24].

Based on the influence of semiconductor structure, no matter what size, its photoelectric performance was always limited. In recent years, the research on the properties and applications of ZnSe nanocomposites was very hot, and this included combining ZnSe with some carbon materials. Stromme et al. [25] fabricated heavy C incorporation at the ZnSe/GaAs would stimulate the thermal relaxation. Cong Chen et al. [26] invested the interaction between ZnSe and graphene-enhanced the optical nonlinearity of ZnSe/graphene composites compared to a single ZnSe or graphene. Kee et al. [27] compared direct hydrothermal synthesis and indirect hydrothermal synthesis of Zinc Selenide/GO composites. Hsieh SH et al. [28] tested the effect of different amounts of GO on the degradation properties of ZnSe/graphene nanocomposites. Tabar MB et al. [29] examined the impact of Selenium-rich and Selenium-poor conditions on photocatalytic properties of ZnSe/rGO nanocomposites. However, there were few studies on the combination of ZnSe nanomaterials and GQDs to improve their photoelectric performance. As a popular material being widely studied, GQDs had excellent properties of other carbon materials, and its high carrier mobility could accelerate the conduction of photoelectrons inside ZnSe semiconductor particles. On the other hand, it could hinder the aggregation of semiconductor particles.

All chemical solvents and reagents used in this work, such as sodium selenite (Na2SeO3), aqueous zinc acetate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O), hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), crystalline flake graphite, ammonia solution (28 wt% in water) and H2O2 (30 wt% in water) were of analytical grade and used without any further purification. Distilled water was used in all preparations.

Flake graphite was used as raw material and GO was obtained through three steps (low temperature, medium temperature and high temperature) by the improved Hummers' method [30]. 1 mL of H2O2 (30 wt% in water) and 0.3 mL of ammonia solution (28 wt% in water) were mixed with 15 mL of GO (about 0.5 mg mL−1), and then the mixture was transferred to a Teflon lined autoclave to heat at 180 °C for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained filtrate was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500 Da) for 48 h [31].

In a typical procedure, 0.22 g of Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O, 0.173 g of Na2SeO3 and 20 mL of 1 mol/L NaOH were treated with ultrasonic vibration, and the mixture was added into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave of 50 mL capacity. Then 10 mL of hydrazine hydrate (80% v/v) and 10 mL of distilled water were added into the mixture during a vigorous stirring. After 10 min stirring, the autoclave was sealed and moved it to 180 °C for 4 h. In the following, the cooled product was washed several times with anhydrous ethanol and distilled water and collected by centrifugal precipitation. The final product was dried at 60 °C for 8 h [32].

Composites were synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal process, by controlling the amount of GQDs, the composite materials with different mass fractions (ZnSe-0.04 wt%GQDs, ZnSe-0.06 wt%GQDs, ZnSe-0.08 wt%GQDs, ZnSe-0.1 wt%GQDs) were prepared.

X-ray diffraction (XRD, Philips, X'Pert 3 Powder) with the Cu Kα radiation system was used to characterize the structural information. Transmission electron, high-resolution transmission electron microscope and atomic force microscope (TEM, Hitachi H-7100, HRTEM, JEOL, JEM2100F and AFM, NT-MDT Prima) were used to observe the morphological images. The elements were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, A Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi). The UV–Vis spectra (UV-5500 spectrophotometer with the wavelength from 190 to 800 nm) and Photoluminescence (PL, Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer) spectra were used to study the optical properties of samples. Fourier transform Infrared (FTIR, Nicolet, IS-10) was mainly used to analyze the situation of functional groups in samples. The zeta potential (ZS90 test instrument made in Malvern, UK) was used to measure the potential value of each sample. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), as well as photocurrent-time response measurements were taken at a CHI650E electrochemical work station.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD analysis of ZnSe and ZnSe-GQDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: The crystalline structure of pure ZnSe and ZnSe-GQDs composites were characterized by XRD analysis. As shown in Fig. 1 , the diffraction peaks at 27.24°, 45.28°, 53.62°, 65.82°, 72.9° and 83.78° could be ascribed to the (111), (220), (311), (400), (331) and (422) crystal planes of ZnSe [33]. All diffraction peaks corresponded well to cubic ZnSe and the lattice constant a = 0.567 nm, which was in good agreement with the standard PDF data (371,463). The results showed that the additive GQDs did not change the crystal structure of ZnSe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology analysis of GQDs and ZnSe-GQDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 showed the morphology and the height profile graphs of GQDs. GQDs were evenly dispersed with the lateral size of 50 nm and thickness of 6 nm. Fig. 2 (d) was the GQDs TEM image, exhibiting a spherical-like morphology with the particle size ranging from 6 to 7 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image showed the noticeable lattice fringes corresponding to the ZnSe (111) plane with a crystal plane spacing of 0.327 nm, demonstrating the formation of crystalline ZnSe phase (Fig. 2 (e)). And the lattice spacing was about 0.162 nm accordant with the graphite carbon source. It proved the successful preparation of ZnSe-GQDs composites. The crystallite size of the ZnSe could be calculated by the Scherrer equation. The Scherrer equation was D = Kλ/βcosθ, where D was the average thickness of the grain perpendicular to the direction of the crystal plane, K was the Scherrer constant of 0.89, λ was the X-ray wavelength of 0.154056 nm, β was the half-width of the diffraction peak of the measured sample, and θ was the diffraction angle. Finally, the calculation indicated that the crystallite size of the ZnSe was 0.394 nm.

XPS results of ZnSe-0.08 wt%GQDs showed that the composites mainly included selenium, zinc, carbon and oxygen in Fig. 3 . The high-resolution spectrum of C could be divided into two peaks at 284.34 eV and 287.23 eV, which corresponded to sp2 hybridized C and CO, respectively. The O 1s spectrum had two characteristic peaks at 531.39 eV for CO and 532.76 eV for C–OH [34], respectively. Of these, the majority of CO and C–OH came from CH3COO− in the zinc source. As seen in the high-resolution Se 3d spectrum, the two peaks located at 53.72 and 54.62 eV could be attributed to Se 3d5/2 and Se 3d3/2, respectively. In the Zn 2p XPS spectrum, two characteristic peaks at 1020.95 and 1044.2 eV agreed well with Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 [35], respectively, corresponding to the bonding energy of Zn–Se.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR analysis of ZnSe and ZnSe-GQDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR spectra of pure ZnSe (a), ZnSe-GQDs (b), GQDs (c) and rGO (d) were shown in Fig. 4 . The curves of ZnSe (a) and ZnSe-GQDs (b) showed an absorption peak at 1610 cm−1, corresponding to the asymmetric stretching vibration peak of COO−, GQDs (c) also has a weak peak here, corresponding to the vibration of C–OH. The peak at 1380 cm−1 was caused by the vibration of CO and the C–O bond [36,37], which was unanimous with the XPS spectra of C 1s and O 1s as mentioned above. The peak of rGO (d) was weak than that of GQDs. The broad vibration situated at 3400 cm−1 of the O–H bonds came from H2O in air.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–Vis analysis of ZnSe and ZnSe-GQDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: In order to compare the optical absorption properties of pure ZnSe and ZnSe-GQDs composites with different GQDs dosage, UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQDs and ZnSe, ZnSe-0.04 wt%GQDs, ZnSe-0.06 wt%GQDs, ZnSe-0.08 wt%GQDs and ZnSe-0.1 wt%GQDs were shown in Fig. 5 . In the UV–Vis of GQDs (Fig. 5a), the emergence of a peak was in the 264.4 nm, which was corresponded to the π-π transition in GQDs. The pure ZnSe sample showed an obvious peak at 446 nm, which was the characteristic absorption of ZnSe. By adding the GQDs, ZnSe-0.04 wt%GQDs, ZnSe-0.06 wt%GQDs, ZnSe-0.08 wt%GQDs and ZnSe-0.1 wt%GQDs presented a distinct peak at 284 nm. Compared with GQDs, the absorption peaks had a blue shift, and this blue shift might be due to the interaction between GQDs and ZnSe. Since ZnSe was known as a direct band gap semiconductor, the band gap values of the samples could be estimated by Tauc's plots according to the direct band gap rule in Fig. 5 (c). The bandgap values were obtained from the UV–Vis results and the intercept method. We calculated the band gap width using working Eg = 1240/λ g, Where the units of λ g and Eg were nm and eV, respectively. By calculation, the band gap width corresponding to different samples was as follows: ZnSe-0.04 wt%GQDs (2.37eV), ZnSe-0.06 wt%GQDs (2.29eV), ZnSe-0.08 wt%GQDs (2.22eV), ZnSe-0.1 wt%GQDs (2.15ev) and ZnSe (2.58eV). Therefore, the band gap energy of the composites was reduced to a certain extent after adding a certain amount of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL analysis of ZnSe and ZnSe-GQDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 (a) showed the PL spectra of ZnSe and ZnSe-GQDs at room temperature with the emission monitored at 380 nm. PL spectra showed that ZnSe exhibited a strong and stable blue light emission peak at 485 nm and an emission peak at 518 nm. In general, the PL spectrum of ZnSe was mainly manifested as near-band edge emission (NBE), that was, the free exciton recombination generated by exciton collisions. The sharp emission peak near 470 nm (2.64 eV) was derived from such near-band edge exciton recombination emission. The difference between the product and the volume gap value might be due to two reasons. One was the quantum constraint effect, and the other was the effect of oxygen in the ZnSe structure. The average particle size of the prepared nanomaterials was near with the ZnSe exciton Bohr radius (4.5 nm). In addition, according to XPS and FT-IR results, more oxygen-containing functional groups were observed, and the materials would have wider band gaps after combining with oxygen [38]. The emission zone at near 525 nm was a deep level emission (DLE), usually caused by defects such as stacking faults, dislocations, or non-stoichiometric ratios of nanomaterials. Masalov et al. reported the emission origin was STS, and the launch of STS was caused by the high surface volume ratio of quantum dots [39]. And ZnSe-GQDs result showed that GQDs could reduce photoluminescence significantly [40]. The charge separation efficiency became higher with the decrease of photoluminescence intensity. Photoluminescence spectra of GQDs with the excitation at 420–480 nm were represented in Fig. 6 (b). When the excitation wavelength was tuned from 420 nm to 480 nm, the launch of the PL peak was redshifted from 507 nm to 530 nm. It showed that the emission peak of GQDs in the emission spectra was relatively broad. The corresponding emission peak could shift to red at different excitation wavelengths with a massive stokes displacement, which represented a mobile energy edge come from the dynamic charge transfer between ZnSe-GQD. This energy shift made the emission wavelength range reach the near-infrared region, in accordance with the less band edge for the GQDs. The dependence of the emission wavelength on the excitation wavelength of GQDs came from the diverse dynamic charge transfer band in the local of surface luminescence microsites [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zeta potential analysis of ZnSe, GQDs, and ZnSe-GQDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: The Zeta potential was measured to understand the interfacial charge of nanomaterials. Fig. 7 showed the Zeta potential of GQDs, ZnSe and ZnSe-0.08 wt%GQDs. The potential of GQDs reached −2.26 mV ascribed to the surface oxygen-containing functional groups from the raw materials which made the surface negatively charged, and the Zeta potential of ZnSe was +2.02 mV. When the GQDs were combined with ZnSe, the zeta potential of the obtained ZnSe-GQDs became −1.72 mV. The positively charged ZnSe and the negative GQDs were electrostatically attracted [18], so it was conducive to their combination. The result showed that ZnSe-GQDs composites had a negative electric potential, which was beneficial for the dispersion of the particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: I-t analysis of ZnSe and ZnSe-GQDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 (a) showed the transient photocurrent responses of ZnSe-GQDs composites (ZnSe, ZnSe-0.04 wt%GQDs, ZnSe-0.06 wt%GQDs, ZnSe-0.08 wt%GQDs and ZnSe-0.1 wt%GQDs). Photoelectrons were generated when a light source illuminated the surface of pure ZnSe. Some photoelectrons could be transferred to form a stable photocurrent, but most photoelectron-hole pairs tended to recombine to cause the low intensity of the photocurrent. To generate strong stability of the photocurrent, pure ZnSe was required to be combined with other conductive components. As shown in Fig. 8 (a), the photocurrent of ZnSe-GQDs composites first increased with the increase of the GQDs mass fraction, and reached the maximum value when the GQDs content was 0.08 wt%, and then decreased with the further increase of the GQDs mass fraction. The result indicated that excessive GQDs provided a large amount of masking to the ZnSe nanoparticles, thus hindering the light absorption by the doped semiconductor. The photocurrent density of ZnSe-0.08 wt%GQDs was three times larger than that of pure ZnSe. It proved that the addition of GQDs could significantly improve the conduction capacity of photoelectrons and effectively prevent the recombination of photogenic electron-hole pairs. The photocurrent intensity was studied by linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) and showed in Fig. 8 (b). It could be seen that the anode current response was increased with the increased positive bias voltage, and the current intensity was greatly improved after the introduction of GQDs with a mass fraction of 0.08%. The result of LSV showed that GQDs was conducive to the separation of electrons and holes, which was in accordance with that of the photocurrent responses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EIS of ZnSe and ZnSe-GQDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: EIS was used to analyze the difference of interfacial charge transfer resistance between different samples. The semicircle at the high-frequency region reflected the interface charge-transfer resistance between the electrolyte solution and the working electrode. The interfacial charge transfer resistance increased with the increase of semicircle diameter. In other words, more charges were recombined, resulting in the current reduction. The EIS spectra of ZnSe-GQDs and an equivalent electrical circuit are shown in Fig. 9 (a). The relationship between the Rs (electrolyte resistance), Rw (Warburg impedance), and Cdl (associated double layer capacitance) and Rct (the charge transfer resistance corresponding to the semicircle in the high-frequency region) was shown in the equivalent circuit diagram. The decreasing Rct value for ZnSe-0.1 wt%GQDs showed a lower charge transfer resistance. With the addition of GQDs, the semicircle radius decreased gradually, which proved that the appropriate amount of GQDs could reduce the charge transfer resistance of ZnSe. Combined with the results of instantaneous photocurrent tests, the addition of GQDs was beneficial to get reduce the interface charge-transfer resistance, promote the efficiency of charge-transfer and improve the photoelectric performance of the samples. The Bode plot could compare the electron-hole separation of the samples. Fig. 9 (b) showed the Bode phase plot of the ZnSe and ZnSe-0.1 wt%GQDs. The photogenerated electron lifetime (τr) could be calculated from the following equation: τr = 1/(2πfpeak). The lower peak frequency for the ZnSe-0.1 wt%GQDs indicated the high lifetime of photogenerated electrons. The results confirmed the EIS results from a more intuitive perspective [42,43].

ZnSe was a relevant wide-bandgap Ⅱ-Ⅵ semiconductor material. It had the Valence Band (Valence Band, VB), the Conduction Band (Conduction Band, CB), and the Forbidden Band of the semiconductor (Forbidden Band, the Band Gap). When the energy of the photon was more than or equal to the gap of the semiconductor, the electron was excited to cross the gap to the conduction band and create holes in the valence band. Since the electrons always tended to be in the lowest energy state and maintained unstable in the high energy of the conduction band, part of the photoelectrons tended to combine with holes and release light or other forms. The other part photoelectrons would be transferred into the external circuit. From the above characterization, the GQD could help to reduce the bandgap of composite and barrier to the electrode, which could speed up the charge separation in the composite.

As shown in Fig. 10 , when ultraviolet light irradiated the surface of ZnSe semiconductor particles, photoelectrons were excited to the conduction band, and holes were left in the valence band. Since electron mass was less than hole, the photogenerated electrons reached the surface first. For naked ZnSe NP, the surface was positively charged, so the photoinduced electrons would recombine or neutralize with some positive charges on the ZnSe NP surface to give light emission or heat, causing some loss for photocurrent. For GQD capped ZnSe NP, the surface charges were negative, so the photoinduced electrons could be multiplied to produce photocurrent. ZnSe-0.08 wt%GQDs reached the optimal photocurrent due to the combined factors of the surface charges and the masking effect discussed above.

ZnSe combined with conductive GQDs with a particle size of 6–7 nm, which provided a more efficient electronic transmission path, and significantly shortened the electrical conduction distance, On the other hand, the photoelectron-hole recombination was reduced, and significantly improved the photocurrent intensity.

In conclusion, ZnSe-GQDs composites were successfully synthesized by the one-step hydrothermal method. In the result of XRD and TEM, the addition of GQDs with the size of 6–7 nm did not change the crystal structure of ZnSe, and the two lattice spacings belonging to GQDs and ZnSe could be observed simultaneously in composite materials. FTIR and UV–Vis test results showed that the composites had been successfully prepared and the band gap energy of the composites was reduced to a certain extent after adding a certain amount of GQDs. The Zeta potential results had confirmed that ZnSe was composited by GQDs, and it was found that the negative charged composite having advantageous to the photoinduced charge separation efficiency in the dispersed particle film. The transient photocurrent responses of ZnSe-GQDs increased with the increase of the GQDs mass ratio and reached the maximum of 0.768 × 10−5A/cm2 at ZnSe-0.08 wt%GQDs. The photocurrent density of ZnSe-0.08 wt%GQDs was three times larger than that of pure ZnSe. A smaller arc radius on the EIS Nyquist diagram indicated that electron-holes were more efficiently to be separated at a larger GQDs mass ratio. GQDs could promote the transfer rate of electrons to the external circuit and short the conduction distance.

Yun Lei: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – Review & Editing, Validation. Yuncui Wu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – Original Draft. Zicong Jiang: Investigation. Zhong Ouyang, Jiaxin Hu, Yuanyuan Lin, Peng Du: Visualization. Bingsuo Zou: Revision

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China No. 51204129 and open foundation of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials, Guangxi University (Grant No. 2021GXYSOF09).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced photoelectric performance of CdSe by graphene quantum dot modification

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Lei, Z. OuYang, Z. Zhang, Z.C. Jiang, Enhanced photoelectric performance of CdSe by graphene quantum dot modification. Mater Res Express. 6 (1) (2019) 015906.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of Ag3PO4 modified with GQDs composites with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.F. Wang, H.L. Wang, A.L. Xu, Z.W. Song, Facile synthesis of Ag3PO4 modified with GQDs composites with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity. J. Mater. Sci. Mater. Electron. 29 (2018) 16691-16701.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stabilization of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by encapsulation inside zeolitic imidazolate framework nanocrystals for photoluminescence tuning

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.P. Biswal, D.B. Shinde, V.K. Pillai, R. Banerjee, Stabilization of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by encapsulation inside zeolitic imidazolate framework nanocrystals for photoluminescence tuning. Nanoscale. 5 (2013) 10556-10561.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gold electrode fused with AuNPs/GQDs showing enhanced electrochemical performance for detection of phenolic compounds

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.F. Li, L. Wang, J.L. Zhang, S.W. Guo, Gold electrode fused with AuNPs/GQDs showing enhanced electrochemical performance for detection of phenolic compounds. J. Electrochem Soc. 166 (2019) B1707-B1711.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene based materials: past, present and future

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Virendra, J. Daeha, Z. Lei, D. Soumen, K. Saiful L, S. Sudipta, Graphene based materials: Past, present and future. Prog. Mater. Sci. 56 (2011) 1178-1271.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of steady-state electrical-heating fluorescence-sensing (SEF) technique for thermal characterization of one dimensional (1D) structures by employing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as temperature sensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Xiang, C.Z. Li, Y.N. Yue, D.M. Xie, M.X. Xue, N.S.
